speaker: interviewer
text: --together, and then, we'll do your pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: I better give you the cord so I-- [AUDIO OUT]. [HUMMING]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I need you to tell me when you want to switch.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 5. Today is September 15, 1995. Interview with Gizel Berman. So Mrs. Berman, can you tell us briefly-- you've been reunited with your husband in the hospital. And now, tell us how you made your passage to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I took him out right away. And we bought cherries on the street. And we were on cloud nine. And we knew we are-- I wanted him to come back to Czechoslovakia. No, how was it? How was it? Anyway, we didn't agree to either way where to go. Oh, I know. OK. I want-- he wanted to go back to Uzhhorod. And I said, no place to live. The Russians are horrible, just as bad as the Germans. And I don't want you to come back. I already had my horri-- that's a piece I can't tell, the experience with the Russians. Ah, too bad. Anyway, and so we agreed we are just going back to our home town because that's what he wanted. And in no time, he realized how horrible the Russians were. And they were cruel to him. And so we just packed and went to west part of Czechosl-- western part to Czechoslovakia and settled there. And it wasn't easy.

speaker: interviewee
text: But fantastic things happened there with the older man in the attic. But anyway, we got an apartment. And I go up in the attic. I never was in one. I never was in an apartment. And there is an old man sitting. And I look. What is he doing? He says, he's a German. He's hiding. All the Germans were expelled from Czechoslovakia. There were about three million of them. They should walk to the border. And he doesn't want to go. And he's dead. And I started to feed him. And anyway, I kept thinking to myself, if Nick would see him, he might kill him. So I won't tell Nick. And 10 days later, Nick is coming up the steps, and I'm coming down the steps. He come up with a full plate. I'm going down with an empty plate. And he says, you knew? You knew? I thought you would kill him. And I thought you would kill him. Well, we didn't kill him.

speaker: interviewee
text: That brought us more together than anything else that we realized of all-- in spite of all the dehumanization, we are still human beings. And we care. And we don't hate. And that was the biggest, nicest thing what happened to us, to-- to help this German man. Eventually, he did have to go back. But it was a different story. And then we got settled in Czechoslovakia. And life, we started to like it. And then there came a new political things. So it got unsafe. And luckily, Nick had a friend who decided-- found us in the Red Cross directory and sent us papers to come to this country. And I love every minute from the first step. And I could support ourself for three years. Never worked before in my life. And I made Nick know I love it. And he was a good sport. But he suffered a lot. He didn't take it so easily that I'm the supporter. But it was just a wonderful experience how we got settled and how we saved money and how we-- Everything what happened was just nice and good experiences. The people were kind and helpful to Nick in the school, to me at the work. And I can't say nothing else bad, that I loved every minute in this country. And I still do. And I just wish everybody else could feel the way I do and not have any hate in themselves and have more understanding. I would like to see all those hateful words kind of disappear amongst people because there is really no need, no need. It's-- it's-- it's-- we can get along if we really want to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why-- why did you choose to move to Seattle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a coincidence. It was a coincidence. He had to go for internship. And Seattle was the first one who replied that they can take him. And we were in Kansas City. And we came, and we never wanted to go away. And we loved it from the day one. And he got the internship. And I got the job in the hospital. And it just-- everything went beautifully.

speaker: interviewee
text: We could live on peanuts. What I earned was no big money. And yet, we could save. Why? Because Nick got every morning in school $0.25, $0.10 to go, $0.10 to come back, and a nickel for a Coke. And if he didn't use up the nickel for a Coke, something at 3:30, he had to save it. So we did save it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Several hundred dollars accumulated in Kansas City, where we babysat for a minister, an Episcopalian minister. So we didn't pay for the apartment. And for the food, we paid. But we cleaned up. So we paid just for-- everything was beautiful, just really. And I can't-- I wish to go just on speaking to and tell about all the positive things I feel, I experienced. And I want to live this way and wish everybody else would think of the good things and not always on the negative. That's my message.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your life as an artist?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very gratifying. I'm a self-taught artist. And I really had more talent than I knew. But then, I did try hard, worked very, very hard. But it always also, just very, very nicely. And I couldn't wish-- And we have a daughter whom we love dearly and gives us lots of pleasure. And we are very close. And so, we are very lucky people, just a wonderful little family. That's right. No hate here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what would you attribute to your survival?

speaker: interviewee
text: What? That I started to make myself goals the first hour in the concentration camp. And I still make myself goals. And if people get up and make a goal-- I always tried, what can I do for myself to make me happy? But what can I do for somebody, to have phone call, a little thing, something nice to make somebody else happy? It's a very healthy, nice way to live. Of course, my husband is my number one [? man, ?] and to make him happy. And I think he is. And I am. And we have just the most beautiful marriage. And-- it's just unheard that somebody should be married for 53 years after what they went through and feel the way we do. And he boosts my morale. I boost his morale. And it works. It's-- but we both are great help for each other. And it's all because of the positive thinking. That's right. I thank you very much for listening. And I'm sorry if some of these little jewel things that I have to leave out, but it's all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to add anything at the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I'm just very grateful. I hope somebody will benefit from your interview. That's my only wish because my life is mine and everybody's life is their very own. So we cannot make other people happy. We can just help them along and show them sometimes the way and straighten out their thinking because it's a lot, what happens in our mind. If we make up our mind we are going to be content and think positive and what good can be done instead wh criticizing, it makes a big difference. Every day can be just beautiful. Today, even the weather is beautiful. You both were wonderful. And thank you very much for being patient. I'm sure it's no fun to listen to somebody with an accent like I have. But that's part of life. It-- it taught you something that there are people with such heavy accent and you still can communicate, I hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope. Thank you. [INAUDIBLE] is here. OK, see, I have to touch you because that's my favorite thing. I really do.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm rolling here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want to make some comments to each other? NICK (HUSBAND): [MUTTERS]

speaker: interviewee
text: All the people I love, I wish them to have such a wonderful life, in spite of what we went through. NICK (HUSBAND): Mm-hm. That's the way I feel. NICK (HUSBAND): Like Gizel said that despite of it, what we went through, we are looking for best. I should say, we are enjoying life at the present time. And even the road behind us was paved with very fearful and treacherous-- Obstacles. NICK (HUSBAND): --ways, obstacle, yes, we are looking for well instead of the short-- the short road, which is ahead of us, will be-- will be-- will be-- will be paved with-- with happiness because now, we have a daughter. We have a son-in-law. Hm, I forgot about him. NICK (HUSBAND): And these gave us so much pleasure that it's very easy-- To be happy. NICK (HUSBAND): --forget the past despite of it, as I mentioned what I think before, that we should not forget the past. I want to thank you very much that you give us the opportunity to express our thought. And all misery does not reflect the-- the misery of the world. It is just a very, very personal thing what we went through. And we don't want to think that the rest of humanity is the same like the Nazis. Very good. NICK (HUSBAND): Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nick, can you put your arm across your chest just so we can--

speaker: interviewee
text: Your number could show. NICK (HUSBAND): Yes, I would like to mention that many people mentioned-- many people said to me, advised me that I should remove the tattoo. I think it would be a crime to remove. I think that this really should remind me, and whoever sees the tattoo on my arm, should remind them that it can happen to other person, the same thing like happened to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, just leave it right there, Nick, for a second. Marvelous. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: When we roll, you're going to say, this is my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead. Just say--

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe this photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this picture is portrait. It's my mother and father just before they got married. This is an engagement picture. And they were both young, 30s. My mother in her 20s. And it was a long, long time ago. They would be close now to 100. I don't know exactly right now. I know the date of their birthday, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: And their full names?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the full name is Margit and Armin Herskovits.

speaker: interviewer
text: What age?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. This is myself, Gizel, at that time, Herskovits, age three. That's at least what my mother told me. This is Bundy, my older brother. He would be, right now, about 80 years old. And he died in a concentration camp just about six weeks before liberation, somebody told us. This is my younger brother, Leslie-- in Hungarian, Lutzy-- Herskovits. And he would be right now about 79 years old. He lived mostly in Palestine. He went there as a young man. And he stayed there until about 20 years ago when he came to this country. And then he died here. This is the house we lived in in Hungvar or Uzhhorod. First, it belonged to my parents. And then, when Nick and I got married and my father was already dead, so my mother gave it to us. And we really enjoyed those few years what we lived there. It still exists. And now, some Russian gentleman lives there without paying any attention that the house doesn't belong to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is my most precious picture because all three of us are together, and a very nice time of our life. We were newlywed. And our daughter, just a few months old, Margaret, and my husband, Nick, and myself, Gizel.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Gizel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is also a very special occasion when my work was featured in the in the Sunday pictorial. And it just so happened that this was a preparation for a show, which was called, 'Gizel Berman's Heritage in Bronze' because I was doing biblical figures. And I didn't do ever since. And I didn't do them before. It just for one little time, I wanted for that show just biblical figures-- Isaiah, Josiah, and the Shofar blower.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is probably the happiest day in our life together with Nick, being together with our new son-in-law and Margaret, Margaret Berman. And our son's name is-- our new son-in-law's name is Douglas Trotter, and happy parents, Nick and Gizel Berman. And they were married about August 10 in 1991.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sonoma, the wine country in California. That's actually the vineyards what you can see in the background. OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold on. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this sculpture is the most fulfilling thing I ever did in my life. And this is the Holocaust Memorial for State of Washington. And in Hebrew, it reads [HEBREW], 'Thou shall not forget.' It's a quotation from the Bible, from Deuteronomy. And what's so ama-- amazing to me, when I found out that it consists of six letters, and I said, this is just really destiny that for six million and the proverb consists of six letters. I did take the liberty, the artistic liberty, and exaggerated the letter L because it reminded me very much of the chimneys in the crema-- of the crematory. But it's very, very close to reality. And-- no, I-I-I-- I wouldn't want it any other way. I just feel very good about it. It was done in lost wax process, casted bronze. And it's 13 feet high in reality, in full size.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you finish it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had it ready in '81. It start in 1981. And it's on the Mercer Island, which is in Seattle or next to it. And it's in the ground of the Jewish Community Center. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
